## Question
Let \(N=2\) or \(N=3\). Assume \(\gamma\in[1,\infty)\) when \(N=2\), and \(\gamma\in[1,8/3)\) when \(N=3\). Let \(\bar\rho>0\), and let the initial data \((\rho_0,u_0)\) satisfy \(0<\underline\varrho\le \rho_0\le \bar\varrho\) for some positive constants \(\underline\varrho,\bar\varrho\), with \(\rho_0-\bar\rho\in H^3(\mathbb R^N)\) and \(u_0\in H^2(\mathbb R^N)\). Consider the Cauchy problem on \(\mathbb R^N\)
\[
\partial_t\rho+\nabla\cdot(\rho u)=0,
\qquad
\partial_t(\rho u)+\nabla\cdot(\rho u\otimes u)+\nabla P(\rho)=\nabla\cdot\bigl(2\rho\mathcal D(u)\bigr)+\nabla\cdot\bigl(\rho\nabla\nabla\log\rho\bigr),
\]
with pressure law \(P(\rho)=\rho^\gamma\) and far-field behavior \(\rho(x,t)\to\bar\rho>0\) and \(u(x,t)\to0\) as \(|x|\to\infty\), where \(\mathcal D(u)=(\nabla u+\nabla u^T)/2\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the global existence and uniqueness of strong solutions for this problem?

## Choices
A. There exists a unique global strong solution \((\rho,u)\) such that for every \(T>0\) there is a constant \(C(T)>0\), depending on \(T\) and the initial data, with \((C(T))^{-1}\le \rho(x,t)\le C(T)\) for all \((x,t)\in\mathbb R^N\times[0,T]\), and
\[
\rho-\bar\rho\in C([0,T];H^3(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^4(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[
\rho_t\in C([0,T];H^1(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^2(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[
u\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)),
\qquad
u_t\in L^\infty(0,T;L^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^1(\mathbb R^N)).
\]
B. There exists a unique global strong solution \((\rho,u)\) such that there is a constant \(C>0\), depending on the initial data but not on \(T\), with \(C^{-1}\le \rho(x,t)\le C\) for all \((x,t)\in\mathbb R^N\times[0,\infty)\), and for every \(T>0\),
\[
\rho-\bar\rho\in C([0,T];H^3(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^4(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[
\rho_t\in C([0,T];H^1(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^2(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[\nu\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)),
\qquad
\nu_t\in L^\infty(0,T;L^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^1(\mathbb R^N)).
\]
C. There exists a unique global strong solution \((\rho,u)\) such that for every \(T>0\) there is a constant \(C(T)>0\), depending on \(T\) and the initial data, and there is a constant \(c_0>0\), depending only on the initial data, with \(c_0\le \rho(x,t)\le C(T)\) for all \((x,t)\in\mathbb R^N\times[0,T]\), and
\[
\rho-\bar\rho\in C([0,T];H^3(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^4(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[
\rho_t\in C([0,T];H^1(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^2(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[\nu\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)),
\qquad
\nu_t\in L^\infty(0,T;L^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^1(\mathbb R^N)).
\]
D. There exists a unique global strong solution \((\rho,u)\) such that for every \(T>0\) there is a constant \(C(T)>0\), depending on \(T\) and the initial data, with \((C(T))^{-1}\le \rho(x,t)\le C(T)\) for all \((x,t)\in\mathbb R^N\times[0,T]\), and
\[
\rho-\bar\rho\in C([0,T];H^3(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^4(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[
\rho_t\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)),
\]
\[\nu\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)),
\qquad
\nu_t\in L^\infty(0,T;H^1(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^2(\mathbb R^N)).
\]
E. There exists a global strong solution \((\rho,u)\) such that for every \(T>0\) there is a constant \(C(T)>0\), depending on \(T\) and the initial data, with \((C(T))^{-1}\le \rho(x,t)\le C(T)\) for all \((x,t)\in\mathbb R^N\times[0,T]\), and
\[
\rho-\bar\rho\in C([0,T];H^3(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^4(\mathbb R^N)),
\qquad
\nu\in C([0,T];H^2(\mathbb R^N))\cap L^2(0,T;H^3(\mathbb R^N)).
\]

## Question
Let $X$ be an equidimensional projective scheme over a field $\mathbb{k}$, let $\mathscr{L}$ be an ample line bundle on $X$, and let $W \subseteq Z$ be closed subschemes of $X$. Suppose (i) the ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_W$ is integral over $\mathcal{I}_Z$, and (ii) the Segre classes satisfy $s(Z,X)=s(W,X)$ in the Chow group $A_*(X)$. Writing $s^i(Y,X)$ for the codimension-$i$ component of the Segre class $s(Y,X)$, and for $\alpha\in A_d(X)$ writing $\deg_{\mathscr{L}}(\alpha):=\deg\! ig(c_1(\mathscr{L})^d\smallfrown \alpha\big)$, which statement is equivalent to both (i) and (ii)?

## Choices
A. It is equivalent to require that there exists an ample line bundle \(\mathscr{M}\) on \(X\) such that
\[
\deg_{\mathscr{M}}\!\big(s^i(Z,X)\big)=\deg_{\mathscr{M}}\!\big(s^i(W,X)\big)
\quad\text{for every } i\ge 0.
\]
B. It is equivalent to require that \(W\) and \(Z\) have the same underlying closed subset of \(X\), and that
\[
\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(Z,X)\big)=\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(W,X)\big)
\quad\text{for every } i>0.
\]
C. It follows from (i) and (ii) that
\[
\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(Z,X)\big)=\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(W,X)\big)
\quad\text{for every } i\ge 0.
\]
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. It is equivalent to require that the alternating total \(\mathscr{L}\)-degree of the Segre classes agree, namely
\[
\sum_{i\ge 0}(-1)^i\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(Z,X)\big)
=
\sum_{i\ge 0}(-1)^i\deg_{\mathscr{L}}\!\big(s^i(W,X)\big).
\]

## Question
Let $N\ge 3$, let $g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ satisfy hypotheses (G1)â€“(G5), and consider any solution $u\in X^1(\mathbb{R}^N)$ of
\[
-\Delta u-\frac{(N-2)^2}{4|x|^2}u=g(u)\quad\text{in }\mathbb{R}^N\setminus\{0\}.
\]
If, in addition, $g$ is HĂ¶lder continuous, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the regularity of every such solution?

## Choices
A. There exists a solution $u\in X^1(\mathbb{R}^N)$ of the equation that belongs to $C^2(\mathbb{R}^N\setminus\{0\})$.
B. Every solution $u\in X^1(\mathbb{R}^N)$ of the equation belongs to $C^{2,\alpha}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R}^N\setminus\{0\})$ for some $\alpha\in(0,1)$.
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. Every solution $u\in X^1(\mathbb{R}^N)$ of the equation belongs to $C^{1,\alpha}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R}^N\setminus\{0\})$ for some $\alpha\in(0,1)$.
E. Every solution $u\in X^1(\mathbb{R}^N)$ of the equation belongs to $C^2(\mathbb{R}^N)$.

## Question
For every positive integer $N$, let $\widetilde{\Omega}_N$ be the paperâ€™s space of prime-detecting quasimodular forms on $\Gamma_0(N)$, let $\widetilde{E}(N)$ be the space of quasimodular Eisenstein series on $\Gamma_0(N)$, and let $\widetilde{S}^{\mathrm{old}}(N)$ be the old subspace of quasimodular cusp forms on $\Gamma_0(N)$; here $\oplus$ denotes direct sum. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $\widetilde{\Omega}_N$ for every such $N$?

## Question
Let $T$ be an exponential o-minimal theory, and let $T_{\mathrm{convex}}$ denote the complete theory of models of $T$ expanded by a predicate for a non-trivial $T$-convex valuation subring $\mathcal O$. Call $T$ transserial if it satisfies the following axiom: for every $n\in\mathbb N$ and every $(n+1)$-ary $T$-definable function $f$, writing $\dagger_n f(\bar x,t)=\frac{\partial f(\bar x,t)/\partial t}{f(\bar x,t)}$ when defined and $\infty$ otherwise, and $G_m^f(\bar x,t):=\min\{|(\dagger_n^k f)(\bar x,t)|:k\le m\}$, there is some $m\in\mathbb N$ such that
$$T_{\mathrm{convex}}\models \forall \bar x\,\exists r\in\mathcal O\,\forall t\in\mathcal O\,(t>r\to G_m^f(\bar x,t)<1).$$
Also, $\mathbb E\langle b\rangle$ denotes the $T$-substructure generated by $\mathbb E\cup\{b\}$, $y\equiv_{\mathbb E} g(b)$ means that $y$ and $g(b)$ have the same complete type over $\mathbb E$, and $\mathbb E^{>\mathcal O}:=\{x\in\mathbb E:x>a\text{ for all }a\in\mathcal O\}$ (so $\mathcal O<b<\mathbb E^{>\mathcal O}$ is interpreted pointwise). Consider the following conditions:
(1) $T$ is transserial.
(2) $T$ is exponentially bounded and, for every $(\mathbb E, \mathcal O) \models T_{\mathrm{convex}}$, every $b$ with $\mathcal O < b < \mathbb E^{>\mathcal O}$, and every $y \in \mathbb E\langle b\rangle$, there exists an $\mathbb E$-definable gne $g$ such that $y \equiv_{\mathbb E} g(b)$.
(3) Every $1$-$\dcl_T$-dimensional wim-constructible extension of a model of $T_{\mathrm{convex}}$ has the gne-property.
(4) Every $1$-$\dcl_T$-dimensional res-constructible extension of a model of $T_{\mathrm{convex}}$ has the gne-property.
(5) Every wim-constructible extension of a model of $T_{\mathrm{convex}}$ has the gne-property.
(6) Every res-constructible extension of a model of $T_{\mathrm{convex}}$ has the gne-property.
Which statement about these six conditions is valid?

## Choices
A. Conditions (2), (3), and (4) are equivalent, and each of them follows from condition (1); however, to obtain either of the full constructibility statements (5) or (6) one needs an additional argument not forced by the first four conditions, so (5) and (6) may fail even when (1)â€“(4) hold.
B. All six conditions are equivalent, and in condition (2) the witnessing \(\mathbb E\)-definable gne \(g\) can always be chosen so that \(y=g(b)\), not just \(y\equiv_{\mathbb E} g(b)\).
C. Conditions (1), (2), (3), and (4) are equivalent. Moreover, each of conditions (5) and (6) implies these four, but the converse implications from the 1-dimensional cases to the arbitrary constructible cases need not hold.
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. If any one of the six conditions holds, then conditions (2), (3), (4), (5), and (6) all hold.

## Question
Let $G$ be a maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph, meaning that $G$ has no $K_5$ minor and adding any missing edge to $G$ creates a $K_5$ minor. Here a $2$-sum or $3$-sum means identifying two graphs along a common clique of size $2$ or $3$, respectively; a maximal planar graph is a planar graph to which no edge can be added while preserving planarity; and $M_8$ denotes the $8$-vertex MĂ¶bius ladder. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of $G$?

## Choices
A. Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $2$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.
B. Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $3$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.
C. Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $2$-sums and $3$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs alone.
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of clique-sums of order at most $3$, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.

## Question
Let \(g\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)\) with \(g(0)=0\). For \(s>0\), define
\[
X^s(\mathbb R^2):=\{f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2):\partial_x^{-1}f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2),\ \partial_x f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2)\},
\]
where \(\partial_x^{-1}f\) is the Fourier multiplier with symbol \((i\xi)^{-1}\). Let \(s\ge 2\), let \(u_0\in X^s(\mathbb R^2)\), and let
\[
 u\in C([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^s(\mathbb R^2))\cap C^1([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^{s-2}(\mathbb R^2))
\]
be the unique solution on its maximal existence interval \([0,T_{u_0}^*)\) of
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t+\gamma u u_x+G*\partial_x\left(\frac12 g(u)+\frac\gamma2 u_x^2-\frac\gamma2 u^2\right)+G*v_y=0,\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2,\\
\nu_y=v_x,\\
u\big|_{t=0}=u_0(x,y),\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2.
\end{cases}
\]
Assume that the maximal existence time is finite, \(T_{u_0}^*<\infty\). Writing \(\nabla u=(u_x,u_y)\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the blow-up behavior of \(u\) as \(t\to T_{u_0}^{*-}\)?

## Choices
A. Necessarily, for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\),
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^{2-\varepsilon}\,d\tau=\infty.
\]
B. Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|u_x(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^2\,d\tau=\infty.
\]
C. Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}\,d\tau=\infty.
\]
D. Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^2\,d\tau=\infty.
\]
E. Necessarily,
\[
\limsup_{t\to T_{u_0}^{* -}}\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.
\]

## Question
Let \(q(G)\) denote the largest eigenvalue of the signless Laplacian of a graph \(G\). Suppose \(G\) is a connected outerplanar graph of order \(n\) (that is, with \(n\) vertices) that is \(C_\ell\)-free, where \(\ell\ge 3\). Let \(P_t\) be the path on \(t\) vertices, let \(\alpha P_{\ell-2}\) mean the disjoint union of \(\alpha\) copies of \(P_{\ell-2}\), let \(\cup\) denote disjoint union, and let \(\vee\) denote graph join. Define
\[
\alpha=\left\lfloor \frac{n-1}{\ell-2}\right\rfloor,\qquad r=n-1-\alpha(\ell-2),
\]
and, if \(r=0\), interpret \(P_r=P_0\) as the empty graph. Which quantitative estimate holds for \(q(G)\)?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\).
C. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup rP_1)\bigr)\), where \(rP_1\) denotes the disjoint union of \(r\) copies of \(P_1\); with equality if and only if \(G\cong K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup rP_1)\).
D. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\), and equality holds whenever \(G\) has the same degree sequence as \(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\).
E. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\), with equality if and only if \(G\) has a dominating vertex \(v\) such that the components of \(G-v\) have orders \(\ell-2,\ldots,\ell-2,r\).

## Question
Let \(N\ge 1\), let \(m\) satisfy \(\frac{(N-2)_+}{N+2}<m<1\) (where \((N-2)_+:=\max\{N-2,0\}\)), let \(\beta>0\), and assume the chemotactic flux has the form \(\chi(u,v)u=\chi u^{\mathfrak q-1}\) for some constant \(\chi>0\) and some exponent \(1<\mathfrak q<\frac{(m+1)(N+2)}{2N}\). Let \((u,v)\) satisfy the system
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t = \operatorname{div}(\nabla u^m)-\operatorname{div}(\chi(u,v)u\nabla v),\\[4pt]
v_t = \Delta v-\beta v+u,
\end{cases}
\qquad \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N\times(0,\infty),
\]
with initial data \(u(x,0)=u_0(x)>0\) and \(v(x,0)=v_0(x)\ge 0\) on \(\mathbb{R}^N\), and assume that \(u\) is a locally bounded weak solution of the first equation. Fix \(T>0\), let \({\mathcal K}\subset \mathbb{R}^N\) be compact, and write \(\|u\|_\infty\) for the essential supremum of \(u\) on the region under consideration. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative estimate for \(u\) on \({\mathcal K}\times(0,T]\)?

## Choices
A. There exist constants \(\gamma>0\) and \(\theta\in(0,1)\), depending only on the data, such that for every compact set \({\mathcal K}\subset \mathbb{R}^N\) and all \((x_1,t_1),(x_2,t_2)\in {\mathcal K}\times[0,T]\),
\[
|u(x_1,t_1)-u(x_2,t_2)|\le \gamma\Big(|x_1-x_2|+\|u\|_\infty^{\frac{m-1}{2}}|t_1-t_2|^{1/2}\Big)^{\theta}.
\]
B. There exist constants \(\gamma>0\) and \(\theta\in(0,1)\), depending only on the data, such that for every compact set \({\mathcal K}\subset \mathbb{R}^N\) and all \((x_1,t_1),(x_2,t_2)\in {\mathcal K}\times(0,T]\),
\[
|u(x_1,t_1)-u(x_2,t_2)|\le \gamma\Big(|x_1-x_2|+\|u\|_\infty^{\frac{1-m}{2}}|t_1-t_2|^{1/2}\Big)^{\theta}.
\]
C. There exist constants \(\gamma>0\) and \(\theta\in(0,1)\), depending only on the data, such that for every compact set \({\mathcal K}\subset \mathbb{R}^N\) and all \((x_1,t_1),(x_2,t_2)\in {\mathcal K}\times(0,T]\),
\[
|u(x_1,t_1)-u(x_2,t_2)|\le \gamma\Big(|x_1-x_2|+\|u\|_\infty^{\frac{m-1}{2}}|t_1-t_2|^{\frac{1}{m+1}}\Big)^{\theta}.
\]
D. There exist constants \(\gamma>0\) and \(\theta\in(0,1)\), depending only on the data, such that for every compact set \({\mathcal K}\subset \mathbb{R}^N\), all \(x_1,x_2\in {\mathcal K}\), and every \(t\in(0,T]\),
\[
|u(x_1,t)-u(x_2,t)|\le \gamma |x_1-x_2|^{\theta}.
\]
E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Let $K_t$ denote the complete graph on $t$ vertices. For graphs $G$ and $H$, write $G\rightarrow H$ to mean that every red-blue coloring of the edges of $G$ contains a monochromatic copy of $H$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about this relation for every integer $k\ge 2$?

## Choices
A. For every integer $k\ge 2$ there is a graph $G$ such that $G\nrightarrow K_k$ and $G\rightarrow (K_{R(k)-1},K_k)$, where $R(k)$ denotes the Ramsey number of $K_k$.
B. For every integer $k\ge 2$ there is a graph $G$ such that $K_k\nsubseteq G$ and $G\rightarrow (K_{R(k)-1},K_{k-1})$, where $R(k)$ denotes the Ramsey number of $K_k$.
C. For every integer $k\ge 2$ there is a graph $G$ such that $G\rightarrow K_{k-1}$.
D. For every integer $k\ge 2$ there is a graph $G$ such that $K_k\nsubseteq G$ and $G\rightarrow (K_k,K_{k-1})$.
E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question

## Question
Let $M,N\in\mathbb{N}$ be such that $\operatorname{lcm}(M,N)$ is divisible by neither $2^4$ nor the square of any odd prime. Let $f\in S_k^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(M))$ and $g\in S_\ell^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(N))$ be nonzero new cusp forms with real Fourier coefficients $a_f(n)$ and $a_g(n)$, where $S_w^{\rm new}(\Gamma_0(L))$ denotes the space of weight-$w$ new cusp forms on $\Gamma_0(L)$. Which statement is equivalent to the condition that $a_f(n)a_g(n)\ge 0$ for all sufficiently large $n\in\mathbb{N}$?

## Choices
A. It is equivalent to requiring that $a_f(p)a_g(p)\ge 0$ for all sufficiently large primes $p$; in that case one must have $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ a positive constant.
B. It implies that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ is a constant.
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. It is equivalent to requiring that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and $f/g$ is a nonzero real constant.
E. It is equivalent to requiring that $(M,k)=(N,\ell)$ and there exists a constant $c>0$ such that $|a_f(n)|=c\,|a_g(n)|$ for all sufficiently large $n\in\mathbb{N}$.
